sections.title: 1 Singular Curves in a Projective Plane
3 Generic Embeddings of Surfaces in RP n

spans.text: We prove two theorems concerning the global behaviour of a smooth compact surface S, without boundary, embedded in a real projective space or mapped to a plane. Our starting point is an analysis of the orientability properties of the normal bundle of a singular projective curve. Then we see how an excellent projection from S to the Euclidean plane gives rise to integral relations linking the singularities of the apparent contour. Finally, given an embedding of S in RP n, we look at the discriminant ,4* of a net of hyperplanes that intersects S in a generic way, obtaining a characterization of ,4* in terms of mod.2 cohomology invariants. 9 Sprmger-Verlag

In this paper we undertake a study of surfaces, developed from a global point of view by considering apparent contours and their duals. Our aim is to illustrate the interrelation between some features of these curves and the topology of the surface to which they belong. We are led, in this way, into a region that has been depicted as "a mixture of integral and mod.2 phenomena" [K].

The three sections of this article are meant to unfold different aspects of the subject. Section 1 concerns general properties of curves with cusps and double points in a real projective plane. Some very appropriate remarks of the referee (unknown to us, but whose qualified criticism has been of great help in smoothing the style and improving the structure of this paper) have stimulated us to redesign this part. We have introduced a new and simple presentation of a few basic topological facts concerning singular projective curves. It may be of interest that the present approach leads to a straightforward proof of a slight refinement (Proposition 3) of a celebrated theorem of M6bius [M6]. Our argument is essentially topological (a discussion of related aspects can be found in [Po]).
In Section 2 we apply to apparent contours our results on singular projective curves, making use of some Morse theoretical instruments introduced by Banchoff and Kuiper manuscripta mathematica

[B-K]. Then we sacrifice a line in the target of our mappings and switch from projective to Euclidean plane. New possibilities are gained, due to the orientation properties that become available. We focus our attention on generic profiles of surfaces: it is well known that an irreducible contour in

can be generated by a surface (which may, or may not, be orientable) of arbitrarily high genus (Fig.1). For this reason, at first sight there seems to be little hope of finding a significant connection between the number of irreducible components displayed by the contour and the genus of the surface.

Our purpose is to show that such a connection exists, provided we count the components in the tight way. Theorem 1 establishes a relation between the genus g of the surface and the number of cusps, double points and irreducible components (suitably counted) presented by its genetic apparent contour. It outlines also a new procedure, alternative to the one described in Proposition 7, enabling us to recover the Euler-Poincar6 characteristic of a surface by looking at its oriented apparent contour in the Euclidean plane (our indebtment with the classical theory of the rotation umber of a plane curve, due to Whitney [W1], is self-evident). For a first application of this result, see [B-D]; more consequences shall be discussed in a forthcoming paper [Pi2].
In Section 3 we consider surfaces embedded in

-dimensional real projective space. We intersect them with a generic linear net of hyperplanes, giving a cohomological description of the discriminant defined in this operation (Theorem 2). The information obtained in this way can be used in order to characterize, by means of duality arguments, the second Stiefel-Whitney number of the surface in terms of the cusps of a generic mapping with values in
(Corollary 2). The reader may suppose there is some kind of affinity between our approach to the topology of a surface embedded in RP

, investigated by means of intersection with a net of hyperplanes, and some well known constructions of complex algebraic geometry. The theory of Lefschetz pencils, for instance, as developed by Andreotti and Frankel [A-F]. However, the technical foundations for the study undertaken in Section 3 rest rather on Looijenga's theory of generic families of functions [Lo] and on John Mather's results on generic projections [Ma].

The first formulation of some concepts introduced in this paper had been outlined in joint work with Alessandro Orlandi [O-P]. Partial results were presented, without proof, in [Pill. On that occasion we sketched a first survey of some literature connected with these subjects.

It must be remembered that there are limitations to the amount of information obtainable from an oriented apparent contour. As it has just been recalled we cannot distinguish, in general, between the contour of a torus and that of a Klein bottle. But even restricting ourselves to surfaces embedded in

, it is a hopeless effort to try to deduce from an oriented apparent contour nonambiguous indications about the way in which different "sheets" of the surface are glued together along the fold curve (Fig.8). This phenomenon was originally pointed out in a famous example, usually referred to as "Milnor's paisley" [P, p.11]. The first complete discussion of Milnor's curve came together with the classification of all non-equivalent immersions of the disk
into the Euclidean plane extending a given immersion of
(the question is related to the "Picard-Loewner problem" [Ti2]). Further developments concerned immersions of more general surfaces into the plane

or to the 2-sphere [F1] (a "polymersion" is an immersion which may have branch points). These results made it possible to classify, by means of combinatorial techniques, the different ways in which the neighbourhoods of the fold curves of a surface can be glued together so that they give rise to nonequivalent projections to

with the same oriented apparent contour [F-T1, F-T2, Wi]. Later work dealt with polymersions into a closed oriented surface of arbitrary genus [F2, E-M] and with the generalization of this approach to maps with non-orientable source or target [Ez]. Two recent contributions are [C-W] and [C1].

is equal to the Euler Poincaré characteristic of the surface, reduced mod.2. This final remark concludes the proof of Theorem 2.

Theorem 2 can be used in order to extend to mappings to the projective plane some classical results on the number of cusps of a generic map. The theorems available in the literature concern maps taking their values in a Euclidean plane [W2, T1, H], as well as in any orientable surface
